title: GTPCNZ
text:
GTPCNZ is a public policy-research and translation package on primary care funding architecture in Aotearoa New Zealand and Australia.
Repository: https://github.com/edithatogo/primary-care-funding-architecture
Website: https://edithatogo.github.io/primary-care-funding-architecture/



A public-data anchored benchmark for exploring primary care funding architecture.
A Quarto website and reproducible report.
A Streamlit dashboard for the modelling and dashboard views, with links to the Substack posts.
A public audit trail of assumptions, caveats, and launch materials.





It is not an endorsed policy position.
It is a public-data anchored benchmark, not a linked-data calibrated or patient-level forecast.
In repo-health terms, the benchmark is public-data anchored and remains bounded by the documented claim boundaries.
It should not be used to claim precise fiscal savings, hospital-demand reductions, workforce effects, or implementation impacts.
Full caveat: This is a public-data anchored benchmark and educational explainer. It is not linked-data calibrated and not a patient-level forecast. It should not be used to claim precise fiscal savings, hospital-demand reductions, workforce effects, or implementation impacts.




The design option explored here retains capitation for continuity, enrolment, baseline viability, and population responsibility. It also tests an uncapped, scheduled, rules-based fee-for-service stream for eligible primary medical activity, controlled through item rules, provider scope, clinical governance, documentation, audit, co-payment protections, and place-based accountability.
Short version:

Uncapped does not mean uncontrolled.





Quarto website: https://edithatogo.github.io/primary-care-funding-architecture/
Streamlit dashboard and modelling views: https://primary-care-funding-architecture.streamlit.app/
Quarto source report: reports/primary_care_architecture.qmd
Streamlit dashboard entrypoint: streamlit_app.py
Dashboard implementation: models/primarycare_model/app.py
Model card: docs/calibration/model-card-v1.7.2.md
Claim boundaries: docs/launch/claim-boundaries-v1.7.2.md
MoSCoW requirements: docs/requirements-moscow-v1.8.3.md
Architecture/design diagrams: docs/design/repo-github-streamlit-design-v1.8.3.md
Repo/GitHub/Streamlit contracts: docs/contracts/repo-github-streamlit-contracts-v1.8.3.md
Dependency policy: docs/dependency-policy-v1.8.3.md
Experimental edge lane: requirements-edge.txt and .github/workflows/dependency-edge.yml
Bleeding-edge scorecard: docs/bleeding-edge-scorecard-v1.0.md
Edge triage note: docs/operations/dependency-edge-triage-v1.0.md
GitHub Actions recovery runbook: docs/operations/github-actions-recovery-v1.8.3.md




Install dependencies:
pip install -r requirements.txt
Run tests:
pytest -q
Check the auditable repo-health score:
python scripts/check_repo_health.py
Render the Quarto website:
quarto render --to html
Run the Streamlit dashboard:
streamlit run streamlit_app.py



GitHub Pages is deployed from .github/workflows/pages.yml.
Streamlit Community Cloud can deploy this app with:

Repository: edithatogo/primary-care-funding-architecture
Branch: main
Entrypoint: streamlit_app.py

If GitHub Actions jobs fail before a runner starts, use docs/operations/github-actions-recovery-v1.8.3.md before changing workflow code.



See LICENSE for the mixed code/content licensing terms and CITATION.cff for citation metadata.

title: Evidence and OIA tracker
text:
This page tracks the documents and data needed to move GTPCNZ from a public-data anchored benchmark toward linked-data calibration.
The tracker is deliberately cautious. A document may be:

publicly sourced and read;
publicly sourced for existence only;
stakeholder intelligence requiring verification;
an Official Information Act target;
not yet available.












Request ID
Topic
Requested document or data
Status
Why it matters
Next action




OIA-001
Capitation formula
Current and proposed capitation rate tables, formula, assumptions and pass-through rules
Not submitted/unknown
Needed to calibrate practice revenue and marginal supply
Submit OIA or update tracker


OIA-002
PHO role/function advice
PHO roles, functions and options advice including H2025067082
Not submitted/unknown
Needed to verify PHO intermediation and place/accountability assumptions
Submit OIA or update tracker


OIA-003
Urgent care advice
Advice on nationwide face-to-face urgent care requirements and evidence base
Not submitted/unknown
Needed to test urgent-care policy-game assumptions
Submit OIA or update tracker


OIA-004
Enhanced capitation/appropriation
Cabinet or appropriation material on enhanced capitation and any hospital-to-primary transfer
Not submitted/unknown
Needed to avoid misstating budget architecture
Submit OIA or update tracker


OIA-005
NPCD/access target
Implementation advice, data dictionary and reporting methodology for NPCD and primary care access target
Not submitted/unknown
Needed for appointment and access calibration
Submit OIA or update tracker


OIA-006
Ambulance alternatives
Conveyance, non-conveyance, treat-and-refer, hear-and-treat and handover datasets
Not submitted/unknown
Needed to calibrate ambulance/hospital deflection
Submit OIA or update tracker


OIA-007
ACC treatment payments
ACC treatment-payment, contract and provider payment data relevant to primary care supply
Not submitted/unknown
Needed to test ACC activity-stabilisation hypothesis
Submit OIA or update tracker



Source CSVs: docs/audit/oia-request-tracker.csv and data/evidence/oia_request_tracker.csv.


OIA responses and linked administrative data are needed before model outputs can be interpreted as linked-data calibrated estimates. The current benchmark is anchored to public data but remains an explanatory scenario benchmark.

title: Evidence and OIA tracker

title: Streamlit dashboard content and presentation contract v1.8.1
Streamlit dashboard content and presentation contract v1.8.1
text: The Streamlit dashboard must be readable by a general audience and safe as a public explainer of model logic. It is an explainer for a public-data anchored benchmark, not a patient-level simulator, implementation-evidence source, or decision-advice tool. The dashboard should surface the modelling and the dashboard itself, then link out to the Substack posts.

title: Streamlit dashboard content and presentation contract v1.8.1
text:
The primary audience is an interested public reader, journalist, policy stakeholder, clinician, public servant or reviewer who has not read the full project.
The dashboard should not assume that the reader already understands capitation, fee-for-service, PHOs, NPCD, OIA or model indices.

title: Streamlit dashboard content and presentation contract v1.8.1
text:
The first screen needs:

A plain-English title identifying the dashboard as a funding architecture explainer.
The full model caveat:


This is a public-data anchored benchmark and educational explainer. It is not linked-data calibrated and not a patient-level forecast. It should not be used to claim precise fiscal savings, hospital-demand reductions, workforce effects, or implementation impacts.


A short reader guide explaining the difference between reference scenarios and toy slider settings.
A short guide to reading the dashboard.
A short outbound link section for the launch-series Substack posts.

title: Streamlit dashboard content and presentation contract v1.8.1
text:
The dashboard needs these sections or tabs:

Dashboard: thesis, claim boundary, model status and interpretation rules.
Modelling: the reference scenarios, selected-game drill-down and key charts.
Substack posts: outbound links to the launch-series reading path.
Toy explainer: educational sliders and dynamic toy-output chart.
Toy parameter dictionary: plain-English definitions for every slider, including what 0, 100 and a high value mean.
Glossary: plain-English definitions of core terms.

title: Streamlit dashboard content and presentation contract v1.8.1

Streamlit dashboard content and presentation contract v1.8.1
text:
The dashboard must surface the post links defined in:

docs/public-site/post-surface-crosswalk-contract-v1.8.2.md

Minimum mappings:








Post
Streamlit destination
Required dynamic component




01 upstream rationing and hospital growth
Start here; Current state; Reference scenarios
Supply versus hospital-pressure plot


02 FFS/capitation/blended funding
Funding models or Toy explainer
Funding comparison toy module


03 marginal supply
Microeconomics lab
Marginal supply simulation


04 formulas do not solve games
Game theory lab
Toy incentive game or best-response simulation


05 current reform pathway
Current state; Reference scenarios
F0/current reform comparator selector


06 uncapping primary care funding
Toy explainer; Microeconomics lab
Activity/payment/control simulation



Later game-theory posts and appendices that are referenced by the public surfaces must have a corresponding dashboard module or guided explainer. They must not remain only as off-dashboard background.

title: Streamlit dashboard content and presentation contract v1.8.1
Streamlit dashboard content and presentation contract v1.8.1
Model card v1.7.2 — public-data anchored primary care benchmark
text:
Public-data anchored benchmark for primary care funding architecture in Aotearoa New Zealand.



v1.7.2. This version incorporates red-team and devil’s advocate findings into the model communication layer.




Compare the relative logic of funding architectures.
Identify which assumptions are load-bearing.
Support public explanation, policy discussion, stakeholder validation and OIA/data planning.
Preserve the public-data benchmark boundary while supporting future precision updates.





Claiming precise reductions in emergency department presentations, admissions or costs.
Producing an implementation business case.
Replacing stakeholder validation, equity review or linked-data analysis.
Claiming that any organisation has endorsed the model.
Arguing that fee-for-service alone is superior to capitation.




Does a hybrid architecture — capitation for population responsibility plus an uncapped, scheduled, rules-based fee-for-service stream for eligible primary medical activity plus place-based accountability — allow lower-cost upstream care to expand safely before need becomes hospital demand?



The current reform pathway — capitation reweighting, access target, National Primary Care Dataset, digital access, urgent-care investment, PHO accountability and separate appropriations — may be sufficient to improve access without a new uncapped scheduled fee-for-service stream.




Uncapped activity without audit, item rules and place accountability is a high-risk scenario.
Co-payments can worsen inequity unless protected.
Scope expansion can fragment care unless tied to shared records, governance and continuity.
Rural access may need capital, workforce, housing, salaried and infrastructure levers as well as item payments.
Accident Compensation Corporation is an analogy for rules-based claims, not a complete model for illness-based primary care.




The benchmark uses 70 parameters across demand, supply, funding, governance, ambulance, hospital, equity, risk and implementation domains. v1.7.1/v1.7.2 tiers these into core, extended and exploratory parameters.




Access and supply indices.
Hospital pressure indices.
Fiscal/gaming risk indices.
Hybrid viability scores.
Scenario comparison and sensitivity outputs.




Public-data anchored benchmark. Not a linked-data calibrated or patient-level forecast.




models/primarycare_model/abm.py provides a completed public-data anchored agent-based layer for patient demand, provider capacity, scope rules, access, equity and gaming/fiscal-risk signals.
models/primarycare_model/sd.py provides a completed public-data anchored stock-flow layer for unmet need, primary-care capacity, ambulance resolution, hospital pressure and fiscal-pressure signals.
Neither layer should be described as a patient-level predictive model unless linked data and validation evidence are added later.




Use the model to ask better questions and prioritise validation. Do not use it to claim final effect sizes.

title: GTPCNZ Interactive Reporting & Visualization Suite
text:
Canonical Streamlit URL: https://primary-care-funding-architecture.streamlit.app/
This is a public-data anchored benchmark and educational explainer. It is not linked-data calibrated and not a patient-level forecast. The Streamlit surface now focuses on the modelling and dashboard views, then links readers to the Substack posts. It should not be used to claim precise fiscal savings, hospital-demand reductions, workforce effects, or implementation impacts.
This project includes a reproducible reporting layer and an interactive dashboard designed to make the primary care funding model accessible to both policy experts and non-specialist readers.


The Quarto report is a reproducible document that combines technical thesis writing with live Python data analysis.


To generate the HTML or PDF version, ensure you have Quarto installed and run:
quarto render reports/primary_care_architecture.qmd




Narrative first: Explains the “Game Theory” of healthcare using relatable metaphors (e.g., video games, subscription services).
Traceable model outputs: Pulls directly from outputs/full-parameterised-summary-results-v1.7.0.csv.





GTPCNZ is a public-data anchored benchmark and dashboard for comparing funding architecture scenarios and reading paths.


Ensure you have the dependencies installed:
pip install -r requirements.txt
Then launch the dashboard:
streamlit run streamlit_app.py




Interactive Sliders: Adjust Capitation vs. FFS weights.
Educational Tooltips: Instant definitions for complex healthcare terms.
Real-time Plotting: Shows qualitative changes in model-generated supply and hospital-pressure index values.
Deployment-ready Entry Point: streamlit_app.py is the Streamlit Community Cloud entrypoint.






Modularity: The dashboard leverages the existing project data structure.
Accessibility: Content is simplified without losing technical rigor.
Transparency: All visualisations are generated from traceable project outputs.
Automated Testing: models/tests/test_app.py uses Streamlit’s native AppTest API.

title: GTPCNZ: The Funding Architecture Report
text:
Calibration status: public-data benchmark, not a linked-data calibrated or patient-level forecast.
This is a public-data anchored benchmark and educational explainer. It is not linked-data calibrated and not a patient-level forecast. It should not be used to claim precise fiscal savings, hospital-demand reductions, workforce effects, or implementation impacts.
The current reform pathway is used as the comparator. Uncapped does not mean uncontrolled.

Executive Summary
Imagine you are playing a video game where you have to keep a city healthy. If you only pay the doctors when they work in the big, expensive hospitals, but don’t pay them enough to work in the small neighborhood clinics, what happens? People get sick in their neighborhoods and then rush to the hospital all at once. The hospital gets crowded, and everyone has to wait a long time.
This report explains a new way to fund (pay for) primary care—the doctors and nurses in your neighborhood. We want to make sure they are paid fairly for every person they help, so that they can stop health problems before they become big enough to need a hospital visit.


1. The Big Idea: “Uncapped but Controlled”
Right now, funding for doctors can be like a “fixed envelope” of money. Once that envelope is empty, it’s hard for doctors to help more people without losing money themselves.
The design option explored here is different: “Uncapped at the activity-envelope level, but controlled at the item level.”

What does this mean?

Uncapped: There is no fixed activity-envelope limit on eligible work. If someone is sick, the modelled design option allows the doctor to help them and be paid for that work.
Controlled: Just because there is no limit doesn’t mean there are no rules. We use strict rules (like a rulebook in a game) to make sure the right care is given to the right people by the right providers.




3. What is “Capitation”?
In healthcare, Capitation is like a subscription service for your doctor.
Imagine you pay a monthly fee to a music streaming app. You can listen to as many songs as you want, and the app gets the same amount of money whether you listen to 1 song or 1,000 songs.

The Good Side: The doctor has a steady income and knows they are responsible for your health all year round.
The Bad Side: If 1,000 people all want an appointment at the same time, the doctor might not have enough money (or staff) to see everyone, because the “subscription fee” doesn’t increase when the work increases.



4. Model-generated comparison: why the “Full Hybrid” option scores highest
The benchmark compares 10 different funding-architecture scenarios. The chart below shows model-generated index scores, not observed outcomes.










Model-generated interpretation:

Full Hybrid Upstream Architecture (highest index score): This scenario combines the “subscription” (Capitation) with a “pay-per-visit” (FFS) system. Inside the benchmark assumptions, it has higher access-index and lower hospital-pressure-index scores.
Current Reform Pathway: This scenario has stronger scores on some indices, but in the benchmark it has a weaker marginal-supply signal when demand is high.




5. Conclusion
Changing the rules of the “funding game” may make it easier for neighbourhood doctors to respond when people need care. The benchmark shows the logic to test; linked data and stakeholder validation are still needed before making implementation claims.

Technical Note: This report was automatically generated using Quarto. The data comes from the full-parameterised-summary-results-v1.7.0.csv file in the project outputs.

title: Claim boundaries and public wording rules v1.7.2
text:
Claim level
Public wording
What not to say




Public document
“Public documents show…”
“This proves…”


Economic theory
“Theory predicts…”
“This necessarily happens…”


Stakeholder intelligence
“Stakeholder feedback suggests…”
“This is established fact…”


Model benchmark
“The benchmark shows the logic of…”
“The model predicts…”


Policy judgement
“I think this warrants testing…”
“Government should implement immediately…”







Current reforms are the comparator, not a straw man.
Uncapped means uncapped at the global activity-envelope level, not uncontrolled billing.
Place-based accountability is core to the proposal.
Equity protections are core to the proposal.
Accident Compensation Corporation is an analogy for rules-based treatment payments, not a wholesale template.
Hospital growth has multiple causes; upstream access is a candidate driver, not the sole driver.
Model outputs are public-data anchored unless explicitly labelled as linked-data calibrated.





Uncapped does not mean uncontrolled; it means scheduled, rules-based, audited and place-accountable.

title: Streamlit dashboard contract audit v1.8.1
text:
Status: historical pass for the fuller v1.8.1 dashboard concept; current simplified dashboard requires a narrower current-state audit.
The current Streamlit dashboard is a simplified public-data anchored benchmark explainer. It exposes Dashboard and Modelling tabs, summary index metrics, model-generated game outputs, and links to the launch-series posts. Rows below that reference glossary tabs, current-state render helpers, toy-chart helpers, readiness charts, or other removed helper functions are retained as historical audit evidence, not as claims about the current app surface.











Contract requirement
Implementation evidence
Status




First screen identifies the dashboard as an explainer
models/primarycare_model/app.py title: GTPCNZ
Pass


Full model caveat appears in the dashboard
Current app text and metric help text identify outputs as model-generated indices, not observed outcomes
Current simplified surface


Reader guide explains reference scenarios versus toy sliders
Historical fuller dashboard helper: render_reader_guide()
Historical evidence


Interpretation rules are visible
Historical fuller dashboard helper: render_interpretation_rules()
Historical evidence


Current reform pathway is explained as comparator
Historical fuller dashboard helpers: render_current_state() and build_current_reform_table()
Historical evidence


Current state includes capitation, access target, NPCD, digital access, urgent care and PHO accountability
Historical fuller dashboard helper: build_current_reform_table()
Historical evidence


Static current-state table exists
Historical fuller dashboard evidence: st.dataframe(build_current_reform_table())
Historical evidence


Static project-status table exists
Historical fuller dashboard helper: build_public_status_table()
Historical evidence


Static architecture diagram exists
Historical fuller dashboard helper: render_current_state_diagram() used st.graphviz_chart()
Historical evidence


Dynamic reference-scenario viability chart exists
Historical fuller dashboard helper: render_reference_viability() used Plotly
Historical evidence


Dynamic supply-generation versus hospital-pressure chart exists
Current app uses a Plotly scatter of access score and hospital-pressure index
Current simplified surface


Dynamic scenario score heatmap exists
Historical fuller dashboard helper: render_reference_heatmap() used Plotly px.imshow()
Historical evidence


Dynamic selected scenario radar/profile chart exists
Historical fuller dashboard helper: render_scenario_profile_radar() used Plotly go.Scatterpolar()
Historical evidence


Dynamic toy explainer chart exists
Historical fuller dashboard helper: render_toy_chart() used Plotly
Historical evidence


Dynamic project readiness chart exists
Historical fuller dashboard helper: render_readiness_chart() used Plotly
Historical evidence


Dashboard and modelling sections exist
Dashboard tab, Modelling tab and summary metrics
Pass


Substack posts section exists
Markdown links to the launch-series posts
Pass


Plain-English glossary exists
Current simplified app retains the sidebar Learn the 'Big Words' expander; the historical fuller dashboard had a Glossary tab
Current simplified surface


Toy sliders are not presented as forecast controls
Sidebar and toy-explainer text say they are educational and do not rerun the full benchmark
Pass


Toy parameter dictionary exists
Historical fuller dashboard helper: render_toy_parameter_dictionary() presented public labels, health-economics meanings, high-value meanings and toy-output effects
Historical evidence


Toy slider scale is defined
Sidebar says 0 means absent/weak; 100 means strong/reliably implemented
Pass


Toy labels are plain policy levers rather than unexplained internal names
TOY_LEVER_DEFINITIONS provides labels such as Payment for extra primary care activity, Stable population-based base funding and Whole-population local accountability
Pass


Outputs are described as model-generated indices, not observed outcomes
Reference chart captions and glossary
Pass


No deprecated Streamlit use_container_width usage in app
Regression test asserts absence
Pass


Public Streamlit URL is documented
README, index, dashboard docs and deployment docs
Pass






Local validation after implementation:

Contract/dashboard tests passed.
Model package compilation passed.
Streamlit entrypoint and dashboard modules passed Python syntax checks.

Remote validation after push:

GitHub CI: success.
Publish Quarto site: success.
GitHub Pages: HTTP 200.
Streamlit app URL: HTTP 200.

title: Public calibration
text:
GTPCNZ is currently a public-data anchored benchmark for explanation and scenario comparison. Linked-data validation is required before using it for predictive, fiscal or implementation claims.










Domain
Data needed
Why it matters




Primary care appointments
Booking, encounter, provider type, mode and outcome
Access and waiting-time calibration


Capitation and payment rules
Rate tables, pass-through, programme funding
Revenue and marginal-supply calibration


Co-payments
Practice fee schedules and patient out-of-pocket costs
Demand and equity effects


Ambulance pathways
Conveyance, hear-and-treat, treat-and-refer, handover delay
Hospital deflection


Accident Compensation Corporation
Treatment claims, contracts, Cost of Treatment Regulations
Cross-funder supply effects


Emergency department and inpatient data
ED presentations, admissions, diagnosis and disposition
Downstream hospital pressure


Workforce and scope
Provider type, FTE, rurality, prescribing authority
Scope-enabled supply


Stakeholder validation
Game scoring and decision weights
Face validity and MCDA







Baseline reproduction.
Temporal validation.
Geographic validation.
Equity validation.
Known-policy-shock validation.
Sensitivity and uncertainty analysis.




Public outputs should use phrases such as public-data anchored benchmark, model-generated index, not linked-data calibrated, and not a patient-level forecast. Linked-data validation can tighten the benchmark later.
